checks.expected_relation: S7 workflows continue using retained baseline surfaces, do not add new anchors, and stay workflow-proof-sized rather than matrix-sized.
checks.failure_meaning: S7 has drifted away from retained baseline scope or silently widened into broader matrix execution.

checks.expected_relation: S7 failure/recovery and fanout workflows reuse the same representative deep closure vocabulary as S2: replay/requeue/verify/governance and fanout composition.
checks.failure_meaning: Workflow proof terms have diverged from representative deep closure truth or fanout has drifted toward sink semantics.

checks.expected_relation: S6 and S7 continue to honor S3 cleanup/stability truth, avoid dirty-state dependence, and keep platform-side corroboration secondary.
checks.failure_meaning: Later-stage truth now depends on dirty state or treats platform-side checks as primary evidence.

checks.expected_relation: Cold-operator evidence surfaces remain sufficient to explain S7 workflows without source-code reading.
checks.failure_meaning: S7 workflows now need source-code reading or author interpretation to be understood by non-authors.

checks.expected_relation: S5 release-consumable bundle remains able to discover S6/S7 truth surfaces through canonical helper/report mapping without implying installer or cloud readiness.
checks.failure_meaning: Later-stage truth is no longer discoverable through the canonical bundle or has drifted toward installer/deployment implication.

checks.expected_relation: Capability domains remain technical, usage remains raw technical fact, governance remains audit/manual-action truth, source contract ids remain technical source truth, and product-cut remains technical readiness.
checks.failure_meaning: Technical truth surfaces are being misread or overclaimed as pricing, entitlement, RBAC, or commercial readiness.
